verses.commentary: What shall we say, then? Shall we go on sinning so that grace may increase? Paul anticipates an objection: If grace abounds over sin (5:20), should we sin more to get more grace? This is a logical but perverse conclusion.

By no means! We are those who have died to sin; how can we live in it any longer? The strongest negation (mē genoito, μὴ γένοιτο, may it never be!). Believers have died to sin. Not dying progressively, but died (past tense). This is positional truth. Living in sin contradicts our new identity.

Or don't you know that all of us who were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into His death? Baptism signifies union with Christ, particularly His death. To be in Christ is to share in His death. This is not about the mode of baptism but its meaning: identification with Christ's death.

We were therefore buried with Him through baptism into death in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, we too may live a new life. Burial confirms death. We share not only Christ's death but His resurrection. New life (kainotēs zōēs, καινότης ζωῆς, newness of life) is the result. Resurrection power enables holy living.

For if we have been united with Him in a death like His, we will certainly also be united with Him in a resurrection like His. Union with Christ is comprehensive: death and resurrection. The future tense (will be united) may refer to final resurrection, but the principle applies now. Death to sin leads to life in righteousness.

For we know that our old self was crucified with Him so that the body ruled by sin might be done away with, that we should no longer be slaves to sin. Old self (palaios anthrōpos, παλαῖος ἄνθρωπος) is our identity in Adam. Crucified with Christ (Galatians 2:20) breaks sin's dominion. Done away with (katargeō, καταργέω) means rendered powerless, not annihilated. Sin's power is broken, not eradicated.
Because anyone who has died has been set free from sin. Death breaks legal claims. A dead person is beyond sin's jurisdiction. Justification is like dying: it frees us from sin's legal power.

Now if we died with Christ, we believe that we will also live with Him. Union with Christ in death guarantees union with Him in life. This is both present and future. We live with Him now by the Spirit and will live with Him eternally.

For we know that since Christ was raised from the dead, He cannot die again; death no longer has mastery over Him. Christ's resurrection is permanent. He conquered death once for all. Death's dominion is broken forever.

The death He died, He died to sin once for all; but the life He lives, He lives to God. Christ's death was a single, sufficient sacrifice for sin. Once for all (ephapax, ἐφάπαξ) means no repetition needed. His present life is completely devoted to God. He lives in unbroken fellowship with the Father.

In the same way, count yourselves dead to sin but alive to God in Christ Jesus. Count (logizomai, λογίζομαι, reckon, consider) means to acknowledge what is true. This is not pretending but recognizing reality. We are dead to sin's dominion and alive to God. This is positional truth that we must appropriate.

Therefore do not let sin reign in your mortal body so that you obey its evil desires. Sin no longer has the right to reign, but it still seeks to. We must not let it. The body is mortal (still subject to death) but should not be sin's instrument. Evil desires persist but need not be obeyed.

Do not offer any part of yourself to sin as an instrument of wickedness, but rather offer yourselves to God as those who have been brought from death to life; and offer every part of yourself to Him as an instrument of righteousness. Two masters, two offerings. We choose whom we serve. Brought from death to life describes our conversion. Instruments (hopla, ὅπλα, weapons) suggests spiritual warfare. Our bodies are tools for righteousness or wickedness.

For sin shall no longer be your master, because you are not under the law, but under grace. Sin's mastery is broken. Under law, sin reigned (using the law to provoke rebellion). Under grace, sin's power is defeated. Grace does not license sin; it liberates from sin.

What then? Shall we sin because we are not under the law but under grace? By no means! The question repeats (v. 1) in different form. Grace does not permit lawlessness. Freedom from law is not freedom to sin but freedom from sin.

Don't you know that when you offer yourselves to someone as obedient slaves, you are slaves of the one you obey, whether you are slaves to sin, which leads to death, or to obedience, which leads to righteousness? Two slaveries, two outcomes. Slavery is inevitable; the question is, to whom? Obedience reveals mastery. Sin's end is death; obedience's end is righteousness.

But thanks be to God that, though you used to be slaves to sin, you have come to obey from your heart the pattern of teaching that has now claimed your allegiance. Past slavery to sin, present obedience to truth. Obedience from the heart is internal, not external. The gospel creates heart-level transformation.

You have been set free from sin and have become slaves to righteousness. Liberation from sin is not autonomy but new servitude. Freedom in Christ is not independence but joyful submission to righteousness.

I am using an example from everyday life because of your human limitations. Just as you used to offer yourselves as slaves to impurity and to ever-increasing wickedness, so now offer yourselves as slaves to righteousness leading to holiness. Paul simplifies for clarity. The principle: what was once devoted to sin should now be devoted to righteousness. Ever-increasing wickedness shows sin's progressive nature. Righteousness leads to holiness (hagiasmos, ἁγιασμός, sanctification).

When you were slaves to sin, you were free from the control of righteousness. A bitter irony. Slavery to sin meant freedom from righteousness, but that freedom was bondage. True freedom is not found in autonomy but in serving God.

What benefit did you reap at that time from the things you are now ashamed of? Those things result in death! Sin's fruit is shame and death. Looking back, the wages of sin are clear: no lasting benefit, only destruction. Present shame reveals past folly.

But now that you have been set free from sin and have become slaves of God, the benefit you reap leads to holiness, and the result is eternal life. New master, new outcome. Slavery to God produces holiness and leads to eternal life. This is the path of blessing.

For the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord. Wages (opsōnion, ὀψώνιον, payment for service) implies what is earned. Sin earns death. But eternal life is a gift (charisma, χάρισμα), not wages. Grace gives what cannot be earned. The contrast is stark: death is earned, life is given.
